text: If ever there was a horror franchise
that lived up to the term guilty
pleasure, it was Final Destination. A
franchise that locked in its formula and
gimmick with the very first movie and
just ran with it. The result is that if
you've seen one of these films, you've
really seen all of them. The main
character has a premonition of some big
disaster that allows them and a few
friends to cheat death only to find out
that death doesn't give up quite so
easily. You knew that none of them were
going to make it to the end credits, but
the real entertainment was trying to
predict how and when it was going to
happen. And watching the Rube Goldberg
style setup for each death. It was a fun
little mix of gory slasher flick and
dark ironic humor that perfectly
understood the assignment took itself
just seriously enough to get you
invested and never asked too much in
return. The series finally petered out
with the fifth movie because even
Hollywood can only recycle the same
formula so many times. But like other
horror franchises from the '9s and the
2000s, it's now been gone just long
enough for people to start missing it.
And that means a reboot. And that reboot
comes in the form of Final Destination
Bloodlines, the sixth entry in the
franchise and the first film in almost
15 years. [ __ ] me, I feel old. And now
that I've seen it, what's it actually
like? Well, I can confidently tell you
that Bloodlines is dumb, cheap,
borderline incoherent garbage. and I
loved it. Honestly, I'd almost forgotten
how much fun these movies can be. And
for all its faults, and don't worry,
I'll get to those shortly. Bloodlines
was a neat little reminder of a bygone
decade when horror movies were a little
less stuffy and pretentious and willing
to just let their audience have fun.
Now, explaining the plot is practically
a waste of time in this case because
watching a Final Destination movie for
the story is like going to a strip club
to discuss your tax returns. Yeah, you
can do it, but neither part is going to
get much out of the experience. But
okay, fine. I'll do it just for you. So,
the movie opens in the 1960s when a
young lady named Iris is on a date with
her boyfriend Paul to a newly opened
restaurant that definitely isn't the
Space Needle. And wouldn't you know it,
a series of unfortunate events unfolds
that ends up destroying the entire
restaurant and killing everyone inside.
Or does it? Flash forward to the present
day and Iris's granddaughter Stephanie
is suffering from recurring nightmares
about the incident. So, she travels back
to her family home looking for answers
in a sequence that feels weirdly rushed
and underdeveloped. The trail eventually
leads her to Iris, who's been holed up
in a cabin in the woods for decades at
this point, living as a complete recluse
so that she can cheat death. See, it
turns out that her premonition at the
start of the film allowed Iris to
intervene and prevent the disaster,
saving hundreds of lives in the process.
But this being a Final Destination
movie, death does what it always does
and slowly killed off everyone who's
supposed to die that night and their
families who were never even supposed to
exist. But by keeping herself isolated,
Iris has basically held death at bay
because apparently it's not allowed to
go after her family until it kills her
first. What are you [ __ ] I mean,
what can I say? This death guy's a good
kid. He plays by the rules. Anyway,
within about 2 minutes of Stephanie
showing up, Iris is out of the picture,
and now death is free to go after her
and the rest of the family. And well, I
think you can probably guess what
happens from there. Like I say, not many
people are going into a Final
Destination movie expecting a complex,
thought-provoking story line, and I'd be
lying if I said Bloodlines bucks that
trend. The script touches briefly on the
idea of generational trauma and how one
person's actions can have repercussions
that last for decades, but honestly,
it's never really explored in enough
depth to make it meaningful. The
characters don't really help with this
problem either. There's a big cast, but
none of them are particularly distinct
or interesting. Stephanie's a pretty
forgettable protagonist, and the fact
that the movie speedruns her intro means
that we never really learn much about
her. By the end, I didn't care much
whether she lived or died, which is a
bit of a problem in a Final Destination
movie. The only real standout for me was
Richard Harmon as Eric, who gets a bit
more of a character arc and actually
seems to be having fun with the role.
So, good on him, I guess. I do have to
give an honorable mention to Tony Todd,
though, who makes his final on-screen
appearance in this movie before he
passed away last year. You can
definitely tell the man was ill when he
filmed his scenes, and I guess he knew
he wasn't long for this world at the
time. Apparently, the director let him
add Lib his final lines as a mark of
respect. And in a film that's generally
lightweight and silly, it's actually a
pretty poignant goodbye for such a
legendary actor. Rest in peace, my
friend. You earned it. But let's be
honest here. This is a Final Destination
movie, and all of this other stuff is
just a side order. The real meat of the
experience is in the death scenes. So,
how does it stack up against the rest of
the franchise? Well, generally pretty
good for the most part. They definitely
didn't skimp on the gore with this one.
And I'll never look at lawnmowers the
same way again, that's for sure. There's
a bit too much CGI at times and a few
too many characters that just get
instak-illed by something big and heavy
landing on them. But overall, they were
pretty entertaining, darkly ironic at
times, and played with my expectations
just enough to keep me engaged. Which is
just as well really because at almost 2
hours, Bloodlines is pretty long for
what it actually delivers. I've got to
be honest, I did find myself checking my
watch a few times around the midpoint
where there's a good 15 or 20 minute
stretch where nothing particularly
interesting happens. And I couldn't
shake the feeling that a tighter edit
would have delivered a much leaner,
faster paced 90-minute movie that
doesn't overstay its welcome, which is
what Final Destination films usually
excel at. Ultimately though, while it
might not be pretty or elegant, I think
Bloodlines is still entertaining and fun
enough to make it worth your time. And
weirdly, it actually got me kind of
nostalgic for that early 2000 period of
fun, unpretentious horror movies that
knew exactly what the audience wanted
from them and were happy to give it to
them. And hey, maybe we could use a
little more of that in
2025. Anyway, that's all I've got for
today. Go away now.
